item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
wow, this sure seems like a divisive article!

considering it's now august of 2020 and there doesn't appear to have been a major rewrite, let me just say that i personally am a big fan of the format screw here. i like how it unconventionally told the story of someone who couldn't stop getting interrupted and who couldn't be allowed to do her job. (i know that feel.)

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If this were a Tale instead of a mainlist article, I would have novoted it, but as it stands now the format screw is overly-confusing and feels unwarranted.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This hits me hard
A lot of people have been saying that Mace and Malone are too unprofessional but I completely disagree. They are higher ups, and that is precisely why they get away with it. I have a slight suspicion that Mace was doing things on purpose with Malone as a cohort (except the breach) but it seems like that probably isn't the case considering the redacted agents appear competent and put Mace/Malone in charge of cleanup actions like retrieving Leslie.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I legitimately have no idea what happened in that. all I understand is that someone kept taking amnestics each week and apart from that I have no clue

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think this works. Researchers would be trained to write the proper style themselves. Yes someone has to write the database software that enforces those styles but they can write / test the software with completely made up data of fake SCP's meaning them don't need clearance or to be amnesticised every week.

Department heads who have clearance would be the ones telling off junior researchers for style issues. Plus even if TCD's did exist as described Mace would know about their existence and that they were meant to edit his documents and not be such an asshole.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: agreeing with the above. concept and formatting are extremely good but the base skip of the lollipop and how it ties into the characters is extremely vague. novoting.

item.title: I love this but novote... for now
item.content:encoded:
This article left me feeling like it was going somewhere big but cut me just dry of the punchline. I don't know how much of a say I have in this, but I'd like to make it known that I am strongly pro-this-being-rewritten. It has something interesting and impactful to say and I'd hate to see it slip away. It just needs a push.

EDIT: I don’t know if anyones mentioned this but I also wanted to add that I’m dying to know why that researcher kept writing the article wrong, and why they were being so mean.

item.title: Re: I liked this a lot
item.content:encoded:
I personally didn't find it very hard to read, in terms of comprehending narrative. I just would have preferred a version where its effects were a little more explicit, such that the reader comprehends how much of a misstep this was for the Foundation. Even some babble about how the lollipop was a spontaneous energy generator whose overflow produced light would have been appreciated. It’s the difference between.

This item could have saved the world.

Although this item functioned as a spontaneous energy generator, it’s properties went undocumented.

Small thing but would have helped the article a lot imo 🤷‍♀️

item.title: I liked this a lot
item.content:encoded: I don't usually disagree with the other commenters, but this time I do. I didn't find this hard to read/understand/comprehend at all. I think the format screw works well and all we need to know is that the lollipop (or whatever it was) could have stopped something bad and, because of a poorly implemented new policy, it didn't. The Foundation learned from its mistake after a redacted number of people (including the poor TCP person) died. When stakes are as high as what the Foundation deals with on the daily, this seems completely within the realm of possibility. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree this probably needs to be a tale, or something else at least. I loved the story and really felt for the characters but it does need some work to make it more polished.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is not a poor concept. Not in the slightest. Yet the execution could use some work. I am only able to get a vague picture of what's going on, such as what the K-class event was. This should be processed further, as the document, while not impossible to comprehend, takes to much time to do so with little to no payoff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate what you're trying to do, but I feel that maybe the format it's currently in isn't the best way to display this. Took me a couple of tries to even comprehend it properly, and even then I was still mildly confused by the layout.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've said this before and I'll say it again:

Applying an extreme threat of important significance to a mundane item or concept isn't going to work properly. There is a jarring disconnect between the average everyday item, to a much larger danger that is bigger than itself.

This item, apart from the already absurd formatting, isn't above that kind of problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this. I absolutely love this. Which is why I feel it needs a rewrite.

One of my biggest insecurities is my terrible memory. I can barely keep in mind the names of people incredibly important to me, much less other information like birthdays and favorite food. The feeling of helplessness as information is being demanded of you that this SCP evokes legitimately made me want to cry.

As it stands, the formatting works for me. It slaps. I like it. The issue I have is that the overarching story is a little ambiguous. How would this lollipop save the world, exactly? Even some hints towards that would be helpful.

Downvoting because I want to see this revisited fresh. I’m always available if you need help or critique.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvote.
I like what you're doing with this, but the format screw doesn't really add anything, and mostly serves to make it hard to read. Also, why would the department notes be stored in the SCP documentation?
As others have said, this would work better as a tale.

The other issue I have with this is that it's primarily a story about incompetent dipshits. If a HR person has the ability to "demote someone to D-class" (which is itself a silly idea and further hurts this), then they should have the ability to know about standard operating procedure in the departments they deal with. Hell, the idea that he would even be allowed to talk to the TCD about her job, despite not being allowed to know about one of the ground level bits of it, is silly.

Like I said, some nice ideas, but would work better as a tale and with more competent characters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is very good but it feels more like a tale to me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea, but the concept is confusing. I see why this isn't getting the best of ratings, I think it's the fact it's confusing. I understand some SCPs work because they're confusing, but I don't believe the confusion works with this.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Feel free to PM me with a link when you are ready. Just keep in mind that I am +8 hours from EST, so I might respond later than you would expect:)

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the encouragement and the constructive feedback!

I've mentioned the peppermint smell in the AutoFormat page. Since the Listpages edit would be a bigger change I'll try that out in the sandbox. May I ask if I might run a revised version past you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi Nykacolaquantum, thanks for reading!

Do you have any feedback that I could act upon? Right now I don't know what it is you found confusing. I did work on this a little bit in the IRC channel, but if it's not there yet, it's not there yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually admire what you're trying to do here. Very bold to attempt this sort of format and its a fresh breath of air from the vanilla essay style scps. But i was very confused when reading this and couldnt really grasp the concept of this scp. would love to see a revised and refreshed version of it in future.

I have upvoted not because the article is good, but because I enjoy what the author is trying to do with it.

We have:
- A new department in the Foundation
- A lesson in importance of the documentation
- A sad story of a technician who was just doing their job

However, the chosen format for the "change logs" and dialogues and even reports makes it so it is kind of difficult to read and comprehend. Perhaps, using offsets would work better for the page. I know there is a guide for it, I will try to look it up.

Edit: here is the guide

Edit2: While you add peppermint in the coding above pages, the pages themselves do not mention the taste of the lollipop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've never been more confused when reading a skip. Novoting.
